subject: Complex matrices; fast fourier transform
concepts: Complex Vectors and the Space C^n: Vectors whose components are complex numbers live in C^n instead of R^n. All the usual vector space rules still apply, but arithmetic must account for complex numbers. Think of C^n as an upgrade of R^n where each coordinate can now be a complex number rather than just a real one.

Length (Norm) of a Complex Vector — Why z^T z Fails: For a complex vector z, the ordinary transpose dot product z^T z can give zero for a non-zero vector (e.g., z = [1, i] gives 1·1 + i·i = 0), so it is NOT a valid definition of length. The correct length squared is z^H z = |z₁|² + |z₂|² + … + |zₙ|², obtained by conjugating before transposing. In plain terms, to measure the true length of a complex vector you must take the complex conjugate of each component first, otherwise lengths can come out wrong or even zero.

The Conjugate Transpose (Hermitian Transpose) z^H: The operation of simultaneously transposing and taking the complex conjugate of a matrix or vector, denoted z^H or z†. Whenever a real formula uses a transpose, the correct complex version replaces it with the conjugate transpose. This is the single most important notational change when moving from real to complex linear algebra.

Inner Product of Complex Vectors: The inner product of two complex vectors y and x is y^H x (not y^T x). It can be a complex number in general, but when y = x it reduces to the real, non-negative length squared x^H x. Think of it as the complex-aware version of the dot product.

Hermitian Matrices (A^H = A): The complex analogue of symmetric matrices. A Hermitian matrix satisfies A^H = A, meaning diagonal entries must be real, and off-diagonal entries satisfy a_ij = conjugate(a_ji). Like real symmetric matrices, Hermitian matrices have real eigenvalues and orthogonal (under the complex inner product) eigenvectors.

Unitary Matrices (Q^H Q = I): The complex analogue of orthogonal matrices. A unitary matrix Q has orthonormal columns where orthonormality is measured using the complex inner product (conjugate transpose). Its inverse is simply its conjugate transpose: Q^{-1} = Q^H. Think of unitary matrices as 'rotation-like' transformations in complex space that preserve lengths.

The n×n Fourier Matrix F_n: A complex matrix whose (i,j) entry (with indices starting at 0) is W^{ij}, where W = e^{2πi/n} is the primitive n-th root of unity. Its columns are powers of W and are mutually orthogonal, making it a (scaled) unitary matrix. The Fourier matrix is the computational backbone of the Fourier transform and is arguably the most important complex matrix in applied mathematics.

Roots of Unity and the Number W: W = e^{2πi/n} is the complex number located on the unit circle at angle 2π/n. Its powers W, W², …, Wⁿ = 1 are the n evenly-spaced n-th roots of unity on the unit circle. For n = 4, W = i, and the powers cycle through i, −1, −i, 1. Understanding W geometrically (as a rotation on the unit circle) makes all the entries of the Fourier matrix easy to visualise.

Orthogonality of Fourier Matrix Columns and Its Inverse: The columns of F_n are orthogonal under the complex inner product, so F_n^H F_n = nI, meaning (1/√n)F_n is unitary. The inverse of F_n is (1/n)F_n^H — just conjugate-transpose and scale. This makes the inverse Fourier transform nearly as cheap as the forward transform.

The Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) — n² Reduced to (n/2)log₂n: The key idea is a recursive matrix factorisation: F_{2m} can be expressed using two copies of F_m, a diagonal 'twiddle-factor' matrix D, and a permutation matrix P that separates even- and odd-indexed components. Applying this recursion log₂n times reduces the number of multiplications from n² to (n/2)log₂n. In plain terms, instead of doing every calculation from scratch, the FFT cleverly reuses smaller Fourier transforms to avoid enormous amounts of redundant work.

Recursive Structure of the FFT — Even/Odd Splitting: Each stage of the FFT splits the input vector into its even-indexed and odd-indexed components (via a permutation P), applies a smaller Fourier transform to each half independently, then combines the results using the identity matrix I and a diagonal matrix D of powers of W. Repeating this recursion log₂n times produces the full factorisation. Think of it like a 'divide and conquer' strategy: break a big problem into two half-size problems, solve each, and stitch the results together cheaply.
examples: Computing the length of the complex vector z = [1, i]: using z^T z gives 1·1 + i·i = 1 − 1 = 0 (wrong); using z^H z gives 1·conjugate(1) + i·conjugate(i) = 1·1 + i·(−i) = 1 + 1 = 2, so the length is √2 (correct).
Constructing a 2×2 Hermitian matrix: place real numbers 2 and 5 on the diagonal; if the (0,1) entry is 3+i, then the (1,0) entry must be 3−i so that A^H = A.
Writing out the 4×4 Fourier matrix F_4 explicitly using W = i (since W = e^{2πi/4} = i): F_4 = [[1,1,1,1],[1,i,−1,−i],[1,−1,1,−1],[1,−i,−1,i]], with the (j,k) entry equal to i^{jk} (indices from 0).

Verifying orthogonality of columns 1 and 3 of F_4 (0-indexed) using the complex inner product: column 1 = [1, i, −1, −i] and column 3 = [1, −i, −1, i]; inner product = 1·conjugate(1) + (−i)·conjugate(i) + (−1)·conjugate(−1) + i·conjugate(−i) = 1 − 1 + 1 − 1 = 0, confirming orthogonality.
Showing all columns of F_4 have length 2 (length squared = 4), so dividing by 2 gives orthonormal columns and (1/2)F_4^H · (1/2)F_4 = I, from which F_4^{−1} = (1/4)F_4^H.

Connecting F_{64} to F_{32} via the FFT factorisation: F_{64} = [I, D; I, −D] · [F_{32}, 0; 0, F_{32}] · P, where P is the even/odd permutation matrix and D = diag(1, W, W², …, W^{31}) is the diagonal twiddle-factor matrix with W = e^{2πi/64}.

FFT operation count comparison for n = 2^{10} = 1024: direct multiplication by F_{1024} requires 1024² ≈ 1,048,576 multiplications; the FFT requires (1/2)·1024·log₂(1024) = 512·10 = 5,120 multiplications — a speedup factor of approximately 200×.

Recursive FFT breakdown: F_{64} → two copies of F_{32} (cost: 2·32² = 2048) + diagonal fix-up D of size 32 (cost: 32); then each F_{32} → two copies of F_{16} + fix-up of size 16, and so on for log₂(64) = 6 levels, accumulating fix-up costs of 32+32+32+…= 6·32 = 192, giving total cost ≈ (1/2)·64·6 = 192 multiplications.
analogy: The FFT matrix factorisation is compared to a 'divide and conquer' strategy: just as a general solves a large battle by splitting the army into independent units that each fight a smaller engagement, the FFT splits one large Fourier transform into two independent half-size Fourier transforms, then cheaply combines the results.

The relationship between the real dictionary (R^n: transpose, symmetric, orthogonal) and the complex dictionary (C^n: conjugate transpose, Hermitian, unitary) is presented as a direct translation guide — each real concept has an exact complex counterpart obtained by replacing 'transpose' with 'conjugate transpose'.

The primitive n-th root of unity W = e^{2πi/n} is described geometrically as a point on the unit circle that is exactly '1/n of the way around', making its powers easy to visualise as equally-spaced points on a clock face.
reviewed_transcript: What if your vectors contain imaginary numbers — does the whole idea of length and geometry fall apart? It doesn't, but you have to play by new rules. In real space, the length of a vector is just the square root of the sum of squared components. But take the complex vector [1, i] and try that — you get zero, which is clearly wrong. The fix is elegant: conjugate your components before multiplying. One times one, plus i times negative i — that gives you 2, so the length is root 2. Perfect. This conjugate-and-transpose operation, written with a superscript H, upgrades the regular transpose for complex spaces. Matrices that equal their own conjugate transpose — called Hermitian matrices — are the complex cousins of symmetric matrices, and they share that beautiful guarantee: real eigenvalues, and orthogonal eigenvectors — where orthogonality is measured using that same conjugate inner product. Master this, and the entire framework of linear algebra — eigenvectors, diagonalisation, Fourier transforms — extends seamlessly into the complex universe.
